walkthrough.title: Fill the Gem Bag
walkthrough.text: Let's get Quartz set up! First, find the cloth bag and all the gem tokens. You will see gems in six colors: amber (yellow), amethyst (purple), emerald (green), sapphire (blue), ruby (red), and obsidian (black). Drop every single gem token into the bag and give it a thorough shake to mix them up. The bag is your mine, and you will be reaching in blindly to draw gems throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Coin Supply and Round Tracker
walkthrough.text: Now gather all the coin tokens and pile them in the center of the table where everyone can reach them. This is the bank. Set the round tracker to Day one. The game lasts five days, so you will move this tracker forward at the start of each new day.

walkthrough.title: Deal Action Cards and Screens
walkthrough.text: Give each player a player screen, which looks like a little mining cart. This is where you will hide your collected gems during each day. Now shuffle the action card deck and deal two action cards to each player. Keep these hidden from other players. Action cards give you special abilities you can use instead of drawing a gem.

walkthrough.title: Choose First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick a starting player randomly. Play will go clockwise from that person. Everyone starts with zero coins. You are ready to start mining! Let's learn the rules.

summary.title: Fill the Gem Bag
bullets: Place all gem tokens into the cloth bag
6 types: Amber ($1), Amethyst ($2), Emerald ($3), Sapphire ($4), Ruby ($5), Obsidian (bust)

summary.title: Prepare the Coin Supply and Round Tracker
bullets: Pile all coin tokens in a central supply
Set round tracker to Day 1 (game lasts 5 days)

summary.title: Deal Action Cards and Screens
bullets: Each player takes 1 player screen (mining cart)
Shuffle action cards → deal 2 to each player
Keep action cards hidden

summary.title: Choose First Player
bullets: Pick a random starting player
Play proceeds clockwise
Everyone starts with $0

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Quartz is a push-your-luck mining game played over five days. Each day, miners take turns drawing gems from a shared bag, hoping to collect valuable crystals. But beware — drawing obsidian causes you to bust and lose everything you mined that day. After five days, the player with the most money wins.

walkthrough.title: Your Three Turn Options
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you must do exactly one of three things. First, you can draw a gem from the bag without looking. Second, you can leave the mine voluntarily and sell all gems you collected that day. Third, you can play an action card from your hand for its special effect. Let's go through each option in detail.

walkthrough.title: Drawing a Gem
walkthrough.text: When you draw a gem, reach into the bag and pull one out without peeking. If it is a colored gem, great! Place it behind your screen and your turn ends. If it is obsidian, you bust. You must leave the mine immediately, return all colored gems you collected this day back to the bag, and collect a consolation prize of two dollars from the bank. You take no more turns this day.

walkthrough.title: Leaving the Mine
walkthrough.text: Instead of drawing, you can choose to leave the mine voluntarily. When you do, sell all gems behind your screen for their listed values. Amber is worth one dollar, amethyst two, emerald three, sapphire four, and ruby five. Take coins from the bank equal to the total. Those gems go back into the bag, and you sit out the rest of the day.

walkthrough.title: Playing Action Cards
walkthrough.text: Your third option is to play an action card instead of drawing. Action cards do things like peeking at gems before you draw, forcing another player to draw, swapping gems with an opponent, or shielding yourself from obsidian for one turn. After resolving the card, your turn ends. You draw one new action card at the start of each new day starting from Day two.

walkthrough.title: End of a Day
walkthrough.text: A day ends when every player has either busted or voluntarily left the mine. Return any remaining gems to the bag and give it a good shuffle. Move the round tracker forward one day. Each player draws one new action card. Then start the next day with the same turn order.

walkthrough.title: Scoring and Winning
walkthrough.text: After Day five is completed, the game ends. Count up all the coins each player earned across all five days. The player with the most money wins! If there is a tie, the player who earned more money on Day five wins the tiebreaker.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: Push-your-luck mining over 5 days (rounds)
Draw gems from bag, sell for money, avoid obsidian
Most money after 5 days wins

summary.title: Your Three Turn Options
bullets: Option 1: Draw a gem from the bag
Option 2: Leave the mine voluntarily → sell gems
Option 3: Play an action card for its effect

summary.title: Drawing a Gem
bullets: Draw 1 gem blindly from the bag
Colored gem → place behind screen, turn ends
Obsidian → bust! Return all gems, collect $2 consolation

summary.title: Leaving the Mine
bullets: Voluntarily stop mining for the day
Sell gems: Amber $1, Amethyst $2, Emerald $3, Sapphire $4, Ruby $5
Gems return to bag, sit out rest of day

summary.title: Playing Action Cards

Draw 1 new action card at start of Days 2-5

summary.title: End of a Day
bullets: Day ends when all players have busted or left
Return remaining gems to bag, shuffle
Each player draws 1 new action card, advance tracker

summary.title: Scoring and Winning
bullets: After Day 5, count all coins earned across all days
Most money wins
Tiebreaker: most money earned on Day 5

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Mine
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first day with four example players. Each player will make different choices so you can see how the push-your-luck mechanic works. Follow along even if you have fewer players. Let's go!

walkthrough.title: Day One Begins
walkthrough.text: It is Day one. The bag is full of gems and obsidian. Everyone has two action cards in hand and zero coins. Player one goes first, then clockwise around the table. Remember, on each turn you either draw a gem, leave the mine, or play an action card.

walkthrough.title: Player One Draws a Gem
walkthrough.text: Player one reaches into the bag and pulls out an emerald. Nice — that is worth three dollars! They place it behind their screen. Their turn ends and play passes to Player two.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Draws a Gem
walkthrough.text: Player two also draws a gem and pulls out a ruby. That is the most valuable gem at five dollars! They tuck it behind their screen, feeling confident. Play moves to Player three.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Gets Greedy
walkthrough.text: Player three draws and gets an amethyst worth two dollars. Not bad. On their next turn, they draw again and get an amber for one dollar. Now they have three dollars total behind their screen. They decide to push their luck one more time and draw again. This time they pull out obsidian. Bust! Player three returns all their gems to the bag and collects two dollars consolation. They are done for the day.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Know When to Walk Away
walkthrough.text: Player three had three dollars worth of gems but got greedy and lost it all. The consolation prize of two dollars is better than nothing, but they would have earned three if they had left voluntarily. The key lesson: once you have a decent haul, seriously consider leaving. Pushing your luck is fun, but every draw risks a bust.

walkthrough.title: Player Four Uses an Action Card
walkthrough.text: Player four decides to play a Peek action card instead of drawing blindly. This lets them look at two gems in the bag before deciding whether to draw. They peek, see a sapphire and an obsidian near the top, and carefully draw the sapphire worth four dollars. Smart play — action cards reduce your risk.

walkthrough.title: Player One Leaves the Mine
walkthrough.text: Player one has their three-dollar emerald behind their screen. They could push their luck, but they decide to play it safe and leave the mine. They sell the emerald for three coins from the bank. Not a huge haul, but it is guaranteed money in the pocket.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's Mistake: Ignoring the Odds
walkthrough.text: Player two already has a five-dollar ruby. That is a great haul for one gem. But they want more and draw again. They pull an amethyst — seven dollars total now. They push again. Another colored gem. They push a fourth time and pull obsidian. Bust! All those gems go back in the bag, and Player two gets only two dollars consolation. They had seven dollars and walked away with two.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Protect a Big Haul
walkthrough.text: Player two's mistake was not knowing when to stop. When you already have a high-value gem like a ruby, you have much more to lose than to gain. The math is simple: if you have five or more dollars behind your screen, the risk of losing it all usually outweighs the reward of drawing one more gem. Protect your big hauls.

walkthrough.title: Player Four Leaves Smart
walkthrough.text: Player four is the last player in the mine. With everyone else gone, they could keep drawing with no competition. They draw one more gem — another amethyst. Now they have six dollars total behind their screen. Rather than risk it, they leave the mine and collect six coins. Day one is over!

walkthrough.title: Day One Results
walkthrough.text: Let's check the scores. Player one earned three dollars by leaving early. Player two earned only two dollars from the consolation prize despite having a ruby. Player three also earned two dollars from busting. Player four earned six dollars by using an action card wisely and knowing when to stop. The conservative but smart player came out on top.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You saw how pushing your luck can pay off or blow up in your face. You learned that action cards give you an edge, that protecting a big haul is smart, and that the two-dollar consolation prize is cold comfort. Four more days to go — keep mining!

walkthrough.title: Keep Digging
walkthrough.text:
Before you start Day two, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics like bag counting and when to leave.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to gem values and action cards.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

bullets: Scripted tutorial with 4 example players
Covers a complete first day from start to finish
Follow along even with fewer players

walkthrough.title: Know When to Leave
walkthrough.text: The single most important skill in Quartz is knowing when to walk away. If you have collected two or three good gems, leaving with guaranteed money is almost always better than risking a bust. A common guideline is to leave once you have five or more dollars behind your screen. The consolation prize of two dollars is a poor substitute for a solid haul.

walkthrough.title: Count the Obsidian
walkthrough.text: The bag has a fixed number of obsidian tokens. If several obsidian have already been drawn and returned by busted players, the bag actually has more obsidian in it since those tokens went back in. But if obsidian has been drawn and not many colored gems remain, the odds shift. Pay attention to how many players have busted — it changes the risk calculation for everyone still mining.

walkthrough.title: Save Action Cards for Key Moments
walkthrough.text: New players tend to play action cards early because they are exciting. Resist that urge. Shield cards are most valuable when the bag is dangerous and you have a big haul to protect. Sabotage cards are best used when an opponent has accumulated lots of gems. Peek cards help most when you are deciding between one last draw and leaving. Timing is everything with action cards.

walkthrough.title: Watch Other Players
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what other players are doing. If two players just busted and returned their gems to the bag, the gem distribution has changed. More colored gems are back in the bag, which might make it safer to keep drawing. Conversely, if several players have left voluntarily with big hauls, the remaining gems in the bag might be picked over.

walkthrough.title: Adjust by Day
walkthrough.text: Your strategy should shift across the five days. On Days one and two, you can afford to push your luck a bit more because there is plenty of game left to recover from a bust. On Days four and five, play more conservatively. A bust on the last day means losing potential game-winning money with no chance to make it back.

walkthrough.title: The Last Miner Advantage
walkthrough.text: If you are the last player still in the mine, you have a unique advantage. No one else is drawing gems, so you can take your time. But do not get overconfident. The obsidian is still in the bag. Being last gives you information (you know what others drew) but not immunity. Use that extra information to make one or two smart draws, then leave.

summary.title: Know When to Leave
bullets: Leave once you have $5+ behind your screen
Guaranteed money beats risking a bust
$2 consolation is a poor substitute for a real haul

bullets: Bag has fixed number of obsidian tokens

Track busts → recalculate risk for remaining miners

bullets: Shield → use when bag is dangerous and you have a big haul
Sabotage → use when opponent has lots of gems
Peek → use when deciding between one more draw or leaving

bullets: Busted players return gems → bag composition changes

Days 4-5: play conservatively, no recovery time

summary.title: The Last Miner Advantage

No competition but obsidian is still in the bag
Make 1-2 smart draws with that info, then leave

walkthrough.title: Bag Counting Fundamentals
walkthrough.text: The strongest skill in Quartz is tracking the bag composition. You know how many gems of each type started in the bag. As gems are drawn and sold, and as busted players return gems, the contents change. Track how many obsidian tokens are in the bag versus total gems remaining. If there are two obsidian in a bag of twenty gems, that is a ten percent bust chance. If there are two obsidian in a bag of eight gems, that is twenty-five percent. Let the math guide your decisions.

walkthrough.title: Expected Value Calculation
walkthrough.text: Every time you consider drawing, calculate your expected value. Multiply the probability of drawing a colored gem by the average gem value, and subtract the probability of busting multiplied by what you would lose. If you have six dollars behind your screen and the bust chance is twenty percent, you are risking losing six dollars (minus the two-dollar consolation) eighty percent of the time you gain roughly two to three dollars, and twenty percent of the time you lose four dollars. Do the math before you draw.

walkthrough.title: Action Card Timing
walkthrough.text: Advanced players treat action cards as insurance policies. A Shield card on a dangerous draw when you have a big haul can swing the game. A Sabotage card forcing an opponent to draw when they have eight dollars behind their screen is devastating. The ideal play is to hold your cards until a single well-timed use creates maximum impact. Never play an action card just because you have one.

walkthrough.title: Aggressive Early Strategy
walkthrough.text: One advanced approach is to push aggressively on Days one and two. The logic: early busts cost you relatively little because you have three more days to recover. If your aggressive draws pay off, you build a big lead. If you bust, the two-dollar consolation keeps you in the game. Shift to a conservative exit strategy on Days three through five to protect your accumulated wealth.

walkthrough.title: Conservative Endgame Strategy
walkthrough.text: On the final day, the math changes dramatically. A bust means losing potential game-winning money with zero recovery time. The optimal play on Day five is usually to draw one or two gems and leave immediately if you get anything valuable. A guaranteed three to five dollars on the last day often beats the expected value of pushing for more. Save your best Shield card for Day five if possible.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Watch your opponents' body language and patterns. Players who always push until they bust are predictable — use Sabotage on them when they have a big haul. Players who leave early with small hauls are leaving money on the table — you can afford to push a little harder when they vacate the mine early. The last player standing has the most information, so sometimes deliberately playing conservatively to outlast others gives you a strategic edge.

summary.title: Bag Counting Fundamentals
bullets: Track obsidian count vs. total gems remaining in bag
Bust probability = obsidian / total gems in bag
Returned gems from busts change the composition

summary.title: Expected Value Calculation
bullets: EV = (P(colored) × avg gem value) − (P(bust) × loss)
Loss = current haul − $2 consolation
Don't draw when EV is negative

summary.title: Action Card Timing
bullets: Shield → save for dangerous draw with big haul
Sabotage → target opponent with $8+ behind screen
Hold cards until maximum-impact moment

summary.title: Aggressive Early Strategy
bullets: Push hard on Days 1-2 when busts are recoverable
Big early leads compound over remaining days
Shift to conservative play from Day 3 onward

summary.title: Conservative Endgame Strategy
bullets: Day 5: draw 1-2 gems, leave if you get anything good
Zero recovery time makes busting devastating
Save best Shield card for the final day

summary.title: Reading Your Opponents
bullets: Sabotage reckless pushers when they have big hauls
Push harder when conservative players leave early
Last player standing has the most information

entries.definition: Yellow gem token worth one dollar when sold. The lowest value gem in the bag.
Purple gem token worth two dollars when sold.
Green gem token worth three dollars when sold. A solid mid-value gem.
Blue gem token worth four dollars when sold. High-value target.
Red gem token worth five dollars when sold. The most valuable gem in the game.
Black gem token that causes an immediate bust when drawn. You lose all gems collected that day and receive a two-dollar consolation prize.
When you draw obsidian, you bust. Return all colored gems behind your screen to the bag, collect two dollars from the bank, and sit out the rest of the day.
Two dollars awarded to a player who busts by drawing obsidian. Small comfort for losing your haul.
A mining cart screen used to hide your collected gems from other players during each day. Gems behind the screen are sold when you leave the mine.
An action card that lets you look at two gems in the bag before deciding whether to draw. Reduces risk by giving you information.
An action card that protects you from obsidian for one turn. If you draw obsidian while shielded, ignore it and keep mining.
An action card that lets you exchange one gem behind your screen with a gem from another player. Useful for stealing high-value gems.
An action card that forces another player to draw a gem on your turn. Can cause them to bust when they would have left safely.
An action card that lets you take a gem from another player's collection behind their screen.
